title: SQL Wildcards
content:
### SQL Wildcards

Wildcard characters are used with LIKE to perform flexible pattern matching. Different databases support different wildcard sets.

---

### Standard Wildcards

| Wildcard | Description | Example |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| % | Matches any sequence of characters | WHERE name LIKE 'J%' |
| _ | Matches a single character | WHERE code LIKE 'A_' |

### SQL Server Specific Wildcards

SQL Server supports additional wildcards for character ranges and sets:

```sql
-- Any single character in the range A-F
WHERE name LIKE '[A-F]%';

-- Any single character NOT in the range A-F
WHERE name LIKE '[^A-F]%';

-- Any single character in the set
WHERE name LIKE '[ABC]%';
```

### Wildcard Combinations

| Pattern | Meaning |
|---------|---------|
| '%ing' | Ends with 'ing' |
| 'A%Z' | Starts with 'A', ends with 'Z' |
| '____' | Exactly 4 characters |
| '__ab%' | Third and fourth chars are 'ab' |
| '%[0-9]' | Ends with a digit (SQL Server) |

### Performance Implications

| Pattern Start | Index Usage |
|--------------|------------|
| 'ABC%' | Can use index (prefix match) |
| '%ABC%' | Cannot use index efficiently |
| '%ABC' | Cannot use index |
| 'A_%' | Limited index usage |

> **Key Point:** Leading wildcards (% at the start of a pattern) prevent index usage, making queries slower on large tables.
codeExample:
-- Find employees whose name starts with 'M' and is at least 3 chars long
SELECT name FROM employees
WHERE name LIKE 'M_%'
ORDER BY name;

quiz.question: Which wildcard matches exactly one character?
Why does '%ABC%' perform poorly on large tables?
